Holy Moley were do I begin?! This game is beyond what I expected! 

For someone who has seen the game being talked about for over two years now, I've decided to give it a try for once. 

And I was not disappointed, the graphics at first seem poor at first, almost making it look like a mobile game add. But once you pick up the gun in the first level, the game is just an endless joy ride. 

By far I've had THY most fun ever playing a game like this, it's very addicting to kill the husks in hell due to there being so many ways with you're large majority of weapons, the speed is very high and intense, it runs smoothly so well, the parry is such a fun ability to use.

But being serious, the boss fights are the main thing, they are so unforgettable being extremely fun to play against, sure some are VERY hard for me such as Gabriel requiring huge skill at parrying which yes there are some things you require to master in this game to progress. But aside from that the bosses are so good! And last but not least, the music, not even going to explain it. Best thing I've ever heard.

As a little note for difficulty, the game isn't too hard depending on the mode, (such as I played on casual) but even with the normal mode being active I'd still struggle in some areas due to some weapons not being too good at explaining what they do, and some levels have certain parts I'd retry twenty times due to too much going on. But either way it's like 3% of the game and it can be ignored. 

In the end 10/10

Played it without giving a ♥♥♥♥ about the story, only to realize the lore is an absolute masterpiece. Getting P-rank on everything absolutely destroyed my sanity, but Cybergrind is still my go-to place whenever I need to clear my head. Peak ADHD dopamine loop. (And yes, the memes are real, this game will either cure your neurodivergence or turn you into a femboy)

This is a mixed positive review - I have played the game to completion in three sittings.
The first sitting was relatively enjoyable, with few cheap deaths. Encompassing act 1.
The second sitting had a lot of novel elements, and some nice surprises, but balance problems started creeping up and some fights started to feel rather unfair. Checkpoints got farther apart, which was frustrating.
The third sitting felt painful and overwhelming at times. The game's difficulty scaled up and the length of each map grew exponentially. This was frustrating on multiple fronts. 

In terms of features, ULTRAKILL is very ambitious. You get at least 15 guns alternate fires in a normal run, 2 types of melee hits, parrying, a grappling hook and a lot of secret interactions between literally all of them. There's also a lot of quality of life movement features. There's also temporary situations which completely and utterly change everything about how you play the game.

Every level in the cast of 30+ in the main story feels visually and thematically distinct, and that even surfaces quite well in the gameplay. There's also a unique challenge in every map that pushes you learn game mechanics you'd otherwise miss.

The visual intensity is quite over the top in standard difficulty. Sometimes I would say it is overwhelming and I need a break, only to tackle it again and again until I win.

Although there are some offending sections with no savepoints, almost every major fight checkpoints you literally a step away from the fight. This lets you very rapidly learn and get better. I have to respect that.

On the technical front, this game has issues. I fell through the floor multiple times and had to reset the map (less than 5 minutes of progress lost, each). A lot of the default options did not click well enough for me. Primarily in the way the game chooses to alternate gun variations. 

Supposedly this game has an entire other set of levels in the line of the 'kaizo' ultra difficult maps. I have not tried nor intend to try those. 

Story wise - ULTRAKILL delivers some beautiful sentences and very good voiceacting in one particular section. Thematically everything feels on point, everything looks equally janky and fitting for the content its trying to go for - a weird depiction of hell and limbo and everything around that. A fake mechanical heaven, nice coverage of blood everywhere. Good divine and horror sections.

Now - would I recommend it? The visual and hand-eye coordination intensity in standard difficulty felt too much for myself. I don't know if I can safely recommend this to most people I know. It can be overwhelmingly a bad time past the middle of act 2. Act 3 is completely out of the ballpark of most people I know. I tend to have high tolorence and I got rather frustrated. FPS junkies can definitely enjoy ULTRAKILL a ton though.

The only thing I can tell the devs is that I hope they consider giving players more space to learn without sub-1-second-deaths in standard. Enemies popping into existence behind me and ending me is not fun, I like to know what got my 2 minutes of fight reset. More checkpoints would be extremely welcome too. Better - separate keybinds for the different hands and auto swap variations and weapons by default would help a lot. Being more direct about weapon features like coin splitting in the description would also be extremely welcome.

The boomer shooter genre has experienced a major resurgence in recent years. Inspired by classic first-person shooters from the 1990s, these games emphasize fast movement, powerful weapons, and intense combat. Among the many modern entries in the genre, ULTRAKILL stands above the rest. Developed by Arsi "Hakita" Patala and published by New Blood Interactive, the game combines old-school shooter design with modern mechanics that make it one of the most exciting and rewarding games available today.

One reason ULTRAKILL is considered the best boomer shooter is its incredible speed. Unlike many modern shooters that focus on realism, ULTRAKILL encourages players to move as fast as possible. Sliding, dashing, and bunny-hopping across levels creates a constant sense of momentum that keeps every encounter exciting.

The movement system is not only fast but also highly skill-based. Players can learn advanced techniques to travel through levels more efficiently and avoid enemy attacks. This creates a high skill ceiling that rewards practice and mastery over time.

Another reason for the game's success is its combat system. Every battle feels intense because enemies attack aggressively and force players to stay mobile. Standing still is usually a mistake, which keeps gameplay engaging and prevents encounters from becoming repetitive.

The weapon variety in ULTRAKILL is exceptional. Each weapon serves a unique purpose and can be used creatively. Players are encouraged to experiment with different combinations rather than relying on a single strategy throughout the game.

One of the most impressive features is the weapon interaction system. Weapons can be combined in unexpected ways to create powerful attacks. These interactions allow skilled players to develop creative solutions to difficult situations and make combat feel fresh even after many hours.

The style meter further separates ULTRAKILL from other shooters. Rather than simply rewarding survival, the game rewards players for fighting creatively. Performing varied attacks, moving quickly, and avoiding damage all contribute to a higher style rank.

This ranking system encourages players to improve their performance. Instead of merely finishing a level, players often return to achieve better scores, faster times, and higher rankings. This greatly increases the game's replay value.

The game's soundtrack is another major strength. The music perfectly matches the fast-paced action and creates an energetic atmosphere during combat. Many players consider the soundtrack one of the best in the entire genre.

Level design also plays a significant role in the game's success. Each stage introduces new challenges, enemies, and mechanics that keep the experience interesting. The levels rarely feel repetitive because they constantly present new situations for the player to overcome.

Boss fights in ULTRAKILL are especially memorable. These encounters require quick reflexes, strong movement skills, and an understanding of game mechanics. Defeating a difficult boss provides a strong sense of accomplishment.

The visual style contributes to the game's identity as well. Its retro-inspired graphics pay tribute to classic shooters while still looking unique and recognizable. The simple visuals also help maintain clear gameplay during chaotic battles.

Another reason ULTRAKILL stands out is its storytelling. While the game focuses primarily on action, it still provides interesting lore and world-building. Players who choose to explore the story can discover surprisingly deep themes beneath the fast-paced gameplay.

The game's enemy variety helps maintain excitement throughout the campaign. Different enemies require different strategies, forcing players to adapt rather than relying on a single approach. This keeps combat dynamic and challenging.

Difficulty options make the game accessible to a wide range of players. Newcomers can enjoy the action without becoming overwhelmed, while experienced players can seek out harder challenges that test their abilities.

The community surrounding ULTRAKILL has also contributed to its popularity. Players frequently share strategies, speedruns, and creative gameplay clips online. This active community helps keep interest in the game strong.

Speedrunning is another area where ULTRAKILL excels. The movement mechanics and level design allow players to develop highly optimized routes. Watching skilled speedrunners complete levels at incredible speeds demonstrates the depth of the game's systems.

Many boomer shooters successfully recreate the feel of classic games, but ULTRAKILL goes beyond nostalgia. Instead of simply copying older shooters, it builds upon their foundations and introduces new mechanics that feel innovative and modern.

The game consistently rewards improvement. Every mechanic, from movement to weapon combinations, offers opportunities for players to become better. This creates a satisfying learning process that keeps players engaged long after completing the main campaign.

In conclusion, ULTRAKILL is the best boomer shooter because it combines fast movement, creative combat, rewarding progression, memorable music, excellent level design, and a strong sense of style. By taking inspiration from classic shooters while introducing innovative mechanics, it delivers an experience that both honors the genre's history and pushes it forward. For many players, ULTRAKILL represents the peak of what a modern boomer shooter can achieve.

What an exhausting video game. I thought I was going to love this as I enjoyed combat 1st games like DMC and the recent Doom revivals. Ultrakill is even more exhausting than Eternal, a game I ultimately enjoyed. I think some of that comes down to the HP system in Ultrakill. As the copy says, blood is fuel. You need to be close to enemies when you deal damage to gain HP back. Unlike Doom which has the quick but invincible glory kills, Ultrakill doesn't give you a second to breathe. I often catch myself in a spiral of taking more and more damage trying to get in close to land a healing shot. I accept that in some ways this is a skill issue, that I also selected the 4th hardest of 5 difficulties for my first playthrough, till it took me half an hour to kill the boss Gabriel (Standard seems like a huge step down from Violence). Dashing all around, jumping off walls, cycling between weapons all with multiple modes, it's relentless. Let me be frank, gamer to gamer. I’m someone who prefers Sekiro to Elden Ring, and Nero to Dante in DMC. There's something about honing in to what makes a combat system work and refining it till it sings that I really respect. Ultrakill throws out every idea it has, and there are great ideas here. The coin, really cool, and what combos I know with it, are really cool. I should probably watch some YouTube guides to learn more, but I want to limit my exposure to this fanbase. 

Ultrakill's absolutely everything philosophy extends to level design as well. Functionally most are hallways that connect to combat areas. That's fine, if a little dull, for what this game is (an arena shooter) but it makes each stage distinguished by its aesthetic qualities alone. There is a variety of aesthetics, but there is no cohesion. I’m not drawn to a style, I don’t like how this game looks, its tone, not that there is much that comes through. I think there are "lore" reasons for this but anytime a game obscures its plot as "lore" I'm checking out. Sorry making it vague that this is a magical land of angels fighting robots doesn't make it anymore appealing as a narrative to me. It’s silly, and the writing in its few cutscenes is more overwritten and pointless than this review. Its meager story moments actively hurt the game.

As much as I don’t like this game, I respect how much of a mess it is. They really went for it and I can see what it is that makes some love this game. Hell. I plan on coming back when it’s done, maybe I’ll even explore some of the secret levels. Will I be more in sync with the game's demands then? Probably not, but maybe!

This is a fast paced shooter with a fast-paced soundtrack and simple PSX inspired graphics. There are many levels and secrets to be found. The game also takes inspiration from games like Quake, and, Devil May Cry.

It is genuinely a great and fun game, and I don't usually like playing that many single player games. And I never got seriously frustrated playing the game while playing on what I believe is the hardest difficulty currently in the game (Brutal), except for LAYER 8. I feel like the game is super fun until the third act, in which it slowly becomes more of an endurance test for me. 

Regardless, I still love and recommend it.
